  ARC-1046        06/05/2023        06/27/2023        SEV-2          Data aggregation calculations in population health module producing rounding errors in decimal precision beyond 4 places   Floating-point arithmetic in aggregation engine using IEEE 754 double precision instead of BigDecimal for financial/clinical metric calculations; cumulative rounding variance up to 0.003% in large datasets   16 days

  ARC-1047        06/10/2023        06/27/2023        SEV-3          API response payloads for bulk data export endpoint include deprecated field names from pre-v2.0 schema                    API versioning logic failed to strip legacy field mappings when tenant was provisioned on v2.3.x; backward-compatibility layer inadvertently included                                                           12 days
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Patch v2.3.2 Details:**

> • **Release Date:** June 19, 2023
>
> • **Deployment to Arcadia Environment:** June 19, 2023 (automatic cloud deployment)
>
> • **Validation Complete:** June 27, 2023 (Arcadia confirmed resolution)
>
> • **Scope:** Migrated aggregation engine to BigDecimal arithmetic for all clinical and financial metric calculations; corrected API versioning logic to properly scope field mappings by tenant provisioning version
>
> • **Impact Assessment:** The rounding error (ARC-1046) produced a maximum cumulative variance of 0.003% across large dataset aggregations. While technically a defect, the variance was well within acceptable tolerances for clinical reporting and would not have produced materially inaccurate results. The API field name issue (ARC-1047) was a cosmetic/compatibility issue affecting downstream integrations that relied on specific field names; it did not affect data accuracy.

**6.3 Software Defect Summary**

  Rounding error (ARC-1046)        SEV-2          Minimal --- 0.003% variance                                v2.3.2      14 days                     22 days

**Key Observations:**

1\. Only 4 of 47 tickets (8.51%) involved actual NexusCore software defects.

2\. None of the 4 defects caused system downtime, data loss, or corruption of patient data.

3\. The most significant defect (memory leak, ARC-1028) was load-dependent and intermittent, consistent with Arcadia\'s characterization of \"intermittent latency issues\" in Dr. Okonkwo\'s March 8, 2023 email to Sandra Bledsoe.

4\. All defects were remediated through standard software maintenance processes. The Section 9.1 limited warranty period ran from Go-Live (January 15, 2023) through April 15, 2023. The first two defects were patched on May 2, 2023, which is 17 days after the warranty period closed; however, ongoing maintenance and support obligations under the MSLSA (for which Arcadia paid \$1,900,000 in Year 1 maintenance fees) independently obligated Meridian to provide bug fixes and patches.

5\. The nature and severity of these defects are consistent with standard post-deployment software stabilization and do not support the characterization in the First Amended Complaint that the NexusCore software was \"materially defective\" or \"unfit for its intended purpose.\"

**[7. Aggregate Analysis and Conclusions]{.underline}**

**7.1 Overall Ticket Resolution Performance**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Metric**                                   **Value**
  -------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Total Tickets                                47

  Resolved Within SLA (All Categories)         31 (65.96%)

  Resolved Within SLA (Meridian-Scope Only)    31 of 35 (88.57%)

  Mean Time to Resolution (All)                4.7 business days

  Mean Time to Resolution (Routine)            2.3 business days

  Mean Time to Resolution (Data Quality)       8.6 business days

  Mean Time to Resolution (Software Defects)   14.5 business days

  Zero-Day / Same-Day Resolutions              3

  Tickets Requiring Patch Release              4

  Tickets Requiring Linden Park Coordination   12
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**7.2 Root Cause Distribution**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Root Cause Category**                   **Tickets**       **% of Total**    **Responsible Party**
  ----------------------------------------- ----------------- ----------------- ----------------------------------------
  Client-side configuration / environment   18                38.30%            Arcadia / Arcadia IT

  User error / training gap                 9                 19.15%            Arcadia end users

  Report template customization requests    4                 8.51%             N/A (enhancement requests)

  Legacy data migration errors              12                25.53%            Linden Park Consulting (Arcadia\'s SI)

  NexusCore software defects                4                 8.51%             Meridian

  **Total**                                 **47**            **100.00%**       
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**7.3 Key Conclusions**

1\. **Software Stability:** The NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module demonstrated strong post-Go-Live stability. Only 4 of 47 tickets (8.51%) were attributable to software defects, and none resulted in sustained system outage, data loss, or material impairment to Arcadia\'s clinical operations.

2\. **Data Migration --- Not a Meridian Issue:** The single largest category of problematic tickets (12 of 47, or 25.53%) is directly traceable to data migration errors caused by Linden Park Consulting, LLC, the third-party systems integrator retained by Arcadia. Section 3.2 of SOW-1 assigns sole responsibility for data migration to Arcadia. Meridian\'s contractual role was limited to advisory consulting (\$1,600,000 of the \$14,700,000 MSLSA value). The execution of Change Order CO-004 on December 5, 2022 --- a \$900,000 engagement for data remediation services to address Linden Park migration errors --- further confirms that both parties understood data migration failures were Arcadia\'s responsibility.

3\. **Acceptance Testing Waiver:** Arcadia did not deliver written notice of material nonconformity within the 30-day acceptance testing window that closed on February 14, 2023. Under Section 5.3 of the MSLSA, acceptance was deemed granted. Arcadia\'s first written complaint was the March 8, 2023, email from Dr. Okonkwo --- 22 days after the acceptance window closed. The issues identified in that email (\"intermittent latency issues\" and \"occasional report generation errors\") are consistent with the SEV-1 memory leak defect (ARC-1028, opened May 1, 2023) and the data quality issues, neither of which constitutes a material nonconformity in the NexusCore software as delivered.

4\. **Declining Trend:** The ticket volume declined from 14 in March 2023 to 5 in July 2023, and all 47 tickets were fully closed by July 31, 2023. This trajectory is consistent with normal enterprise software post-deployment stabilization and contradicts the complaint\'s characterization of an ongoing, systemic product failure.

5\. **Damages Attribution:** The support ticket record does not support Arcadia\'s claim of \$47,300,000 in damages. The vast majority of issues were routine post-deployment items or were caused by Arcadia\'s own third-party vendor. The 4 software defects were patched promptly and had minimal operational impact.

**[8. Appendix: Complete Ticket Log]{.underline}**

The complete ticket log with full resolution notes, email correspondence, and engineering analysis for all 47 tickets is maintained in Meridian\'s Zenith Service Management Platform under Arcadia account ID ARC-HC-2022-0315. Full export has been preserved pursuant to the litigation hold notice issued August 15, 2023, and produced to Stonebridge & Calloway LLP as MERID-SUPP-000001 through MERID-SUPP-002847 (2,847 pages).

**[2. EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW]{.underline}**

This report provides a comprehensive assessment of the NexusCore implementation for Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\") as of August 25, 2022. The project is currently tracking **137 calendar days behind the original Go-Live target of September 1, 2022**, and the Implementation Services team has concluded that the original Go-Live date is no longer achievable. This report identifies three primary root causes for the delay, each of which is attributable to client-side or third-party dependencies outside Meridian\'s scope of control. A revised timeline with a proposed Go-Live date of January 15, 2023, is presented below, together with risk mitigation actions and escalation recommendations.

All three root causes were previously escalated to the client through weekly status meeting minutes (references: WSM-012 dated May 5, 2022; WSM-016 dated June 2, 2022; WSM-022 dated July 14, 2022; WSM-026 dated August 11, 2022). This report consolidates those escalations into a single formal document for internal executive review and project governance purposes.

**[3. OVERALL STATUS DASHBOARD]{.underline}**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Workstream**                     **Status**                  **Owner**                            **Notes**
  ---------------------------------- --------------------------- ------------------------------------ -----------------------------------------------
  Requirements Gathering             **GREEN --- Complete**      Meridian / Arcadia                   Completed June 30, 2022 (delayed from May 15)

  System Configuration               **AMBER --- In Progress**   Meridian                             70% complete; blocked on API specs

  UI Customization                   **GREEN --- On Track**      Meridian                             85% complete

  Report Template Development        **AMBER --- Delayed**       Meridian                             Dependent on configuration completion

  API Integration (EHR Connectors)   **RED --- Critical**        Meridian (build) / Arcadia (specs)   Blocked; see Root Cause #2

  Data Migration                     **RED --- Critical**        Arcadia / Linden Park Consulting     Script failures; see Root Cause #3

  Training                           **AMBER --- Not Started**   Meridian                             Cannot commence until configuration stable

  User Acceptance Testing            **RED --- Deferred**        Arcadia                              Dependent on all upstream workstreams
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Legend:**

> • **GREEN** --- On track or complete; no action required.
>
> • **AMBER** --- At risk; mitigation in progress; management attention needed.
>
> • **RED** --- Critical; off-track; executive escalation required.

**[4. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**4.1 Root Cause #1: Client Project Manager Designation Gap (45 Days)**

**Impact Rating:** HIGH

**Description:** Per Section 4(a) of SOW-1, Arcadia was required to \"designate a Project Manager with decision-making authority\" to serve as the primary point of coordination for the implementation. Arcadia\'s original Project Manager, Kevin Liu, departed Arcadia\'s employment on **April 28, 2022**, during the critical requirements-gathering phase. Arcadia did not designate a replacement Project Manager until **June 12, 2022**, when Priya Nair was assigned to the role --- a gap of **45 calendar days**.

**Impact to Schedule:** During the 45-day vacancy, Meridian\'s implementation team was unable to obtain timely decisions on requirements prioritization, configuration choices, or access authorizations. Specifically:

> • Fourteen (14) requirements decision requests submitted by Meridian between April 29 and June 11 went unanswered or were routed to personnel without decision-making authority.
>
> • Access to Arcadia\'s staging EHR environment was delayed by 22 days because no authorized client representative was available to approve Meridian\'s security credential request.
>
> • Two scheduled requirements workshops (May 4 and May 18) were cancelled by Arcadia due to the PM vacancy.

**Schedule Impact:** The 45-day PM gap directly caused a **38-day delay** to the requirements-gathering phase (accounting for partial overlap with other activities). This delay cascaded into the configuration and integration workstreams, as configuration cannot commence on modules where requirements remain unvalidated.

**Meridian\'s Mitigation Efforts:** Meridian escalated the PM vacancy issue in writing on May 5, 2022 (WSM-012), May 19, 2022 (email from Ryan Tanaka to Kevin Liu\'s former supervisor), and June 2, 2022 (WSM-016). Upon Ms. Nair\'s designation on June 12, Meridian conducted an accelerated requirements validation sprint over a two-week period to minimize further delay.

**4.2 Root Cause #2: Late Delivery of API Specifications by Arcadia (75 Days)**

**Impact Rating:** CRITICAL

**Description:** Section 3.1(d) of SOW-1 establishes a shared responsibility for API integration between NexusCore and Arcadia\'s existing EHR systems: Meridian is responsible for building the API connectors, and Arcadia is responsible for \"providing complete API specifications and access to test environments\" per the milestone schedule. The SOW-1 milestone schedule (Exhibit B) required Arcadia to deliver complete API specifications for all in-scope EHR systems by **May 6, 2022**.

Arcadia did not deliver the API specifications until **July 20, 2022** --- a delay of **75 calendar days** from the contractual milestone date.

**Impact to Schedule:** The API integration workstream is on the critical path. Without API specifications, Meridian\'s engineering team cannot design, build, or test the EHR connectors. The 75-day delay to this dependency has the following downstream effects:

> • API connector development, originally scheduled for May 7 -- July 15, 2022, cannot commence until specifications are reviewed and validated (estimated 2-week review period following July 20 delivery).
>
> • Integration testing, originally scheduled for July 16 -- August 15, 2022, is now estimated for November 2022 at the earliest.
>
> • User Acceptance Testing, which requires functional API integration, is deferred from August 2022 to December 2022 / January 2023.

**Contributing Factors:** The late API specification delivery appears to be connected to Root Cause #1. During the 45-day PM gap, Arcadia\'s technical staff indicated they lacked authorization to share certain system architecture documentation with Meridian. Additionally, Arcadia\'s CTO, Martin Schreiber, informed Meridian during the July 14 status meeting (WSM-022) that Arcadia\'s legacy HealthBridge 4.0 platform had undocumented API endpoints that required reverse-engineering before specifications could be finalized.

**Meridian\'s Mitigation Efforts:** Meridian submitted formal milestone reminder notices on May 6, May 20, June 6, and July 1, 2022. Meridian offered to provide supplemental API discovery consulting (outside the SOW-1 scope) at additional cost, but Arcadia declined. Following receipt of specifications on July 20, Meridian\'s API engineering team has been assigned to an accelerated review track.

**4.3 Root Cause #3: Linden Park Consulting Data Migration Script Errors**

**Impact Rating:** CRITICAL

**Description:** Per Section 3.2 of SOW-1, Arcadia is \"solely responsible for all data migration activities, including extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data into NexusCore.\" Meridian\'s role is limited to providing \"advisory consulting services as described in Exhibit C\" and has \"no responsibility for the accuracy, completeness, or integrity of migrated data.\" Arcadia retained Linden Park Consulting, LLC (\"Linden Park\"), a third-party systems integrator based in Houston, Texas, on **July 1, 2022**, to perform data migration from the HealthBridge 4.0 platform to NexusCore.

On **August 12, 2022**, Linden Park executed its first full data migration test run into Meridian\'s NexusCore staging environment. The test revealed **critical errors** in Linden Park\'s extraction-transformation-load (\"ETL\") scripts:

> • **Data Type Mismatches:** Approximately 34% of migrated clinical encounter records contained incorrectly mapped data types, causing field-level validation failures in NexusCore\'s data ingestion layer.
>
> • **Referential Integrity Violations:** Patient-provider relationship tables lost foreign key associations during transformation, resulting in orphaned records.
>
> • **Character Encoding Errors:** Legacy HealthBridge 4.0 data stored in Windows-1252 encoding was not properly converted to UTF-8, causing corruption of special characters in approximately 12,000 patient records.
>
> • **Duplicate Record Creation:** The ETL script lacked deduplication logic, resulting in the creation of approximately 8,500 duplicate patient demographic records.

**Impact to Schedule:** Data migration is a prerequisite for meaningful User Acceptance Testing and Go-Live. The August 12 test run failure requires Linden Park to remediate its ETL scripts and re-execute the migration. Based on Linden Park\'s preliminary remediation timeline communicated to Arcadia on August 19, 2022, a corrected migration test run is not expected before **late October 2022**, with a production migration targeted for **December 2022**.

**Meridian\'s Advisory Role:** Meridian\'s data migration advisory consultant, Elena Vasquez, provided Linden Park with NexusCore\'s data import specifications, schema documentation, and field-mapping templates on July 15, 2022 --- within 14 days of Linden Park\'s engagement, as required by Exhibit C. Meridian\'s advisory scope does not include writing, reviewing, or validating Linden Park\'s ETL scripts.

**[9. RECOMMENDATIONS]{.underline}**

Based on the analysis presented in this report, the Implementation Services team makes the following recommendations:

1\. **Formal Escalation to Arcadia Leadership.** Sandra Bledsoe should deliver a formal written notice to Dr. Rachel Okonkwo documenting the revised Go-Live date and the three root causes, with specific reference to Arcadia\'s contractual obligations under SOW-1, Sections 3.2 and 4. This communication should be reviewed by General Counsel prior to delivery.

2\. **Change Order Preparation.** The Commercial Team should prepare Change Orders in advance for (a) any additional API connectors required for legacy systems not identified in the original scope, and (b) optional data remediation services to address Linden Park\'s ETL script failures, estimated at \$900,000. These Change Orders should be executed per Section 5.4 of the MSLSA.

3\. **Documentation Preservation.** All project correspondence, status meeting minutes, milestone reminder notices, and Linden Park-related communications should be archived in Meridian\'s project governance repository. Given the magnitude of the schedule deviation and the potential for client dissatisfaction, a comprehensive documentation trail is essential.

4\. **Weekly Linden Park Migration Reviews.** Meridian should request that Arcadia provide weekly written updates on Linden Park\'s ETL remediation progress. If Linden Park cannot deliver a successful test migration by late October 2022, the revised Go-Live date of January 15, 2023, will be at risk.

5\. **Internal Legal Review.** General Counsel should review this report and the relevant contractual provisions to confirm Meridian\'s position regarding the allocation of responsibility for the Go-Live delay, particularly with respect to data migration obligations under SOW-1, Section 3.2.

**[10. APPROVAL]{.underline}**

**EXHIBIT D**

**SELECTED EMAIL CORRESPONDENCE** **MSLSA CONTRACT NEGOTIATION PERIOD** **FEBRUARY 22, 2022 -- MARCH 14, 2022**

*Produced by Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.* *Bates Nos. MCS-00004217 through MCS-00004248*

**FROM:** Jonathan Breckenridge \\\<jbreckenridge@wexfordhale.com\\\> **TO:** David Arsenault \\\<darsenault@stonebridgecalloway.com\\\> **CC:** Dr. Rachel Okonkwo \\\<rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com\\\>; Martin Schreiber \\\<mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com\\\> **DATE:** February 22, 2022, 9:14 AM CST **SUBJECT:** Arcadia Health Systems / Meridian Cloud Solutions --- Draft MSLSA Review **ATTACHMENTS:** Arcadia\\*MSLSA\\*Redline\\\_v1.docx (148 KB)

David,

Good morning. Our firm has been engaged by Arcadia Health Systems, LLC to represent it in connection with the proposed Master Software License and Services Agreement and Statement of Work with your client, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. I understand from Dr. Okonkwo and Martin Schreiber that you will be handling this matter on the Meridian side under Meg Calloway\'s supervision. Please confirm.

Attached is our initial redline of the draft MSLSA that Meridian circulated on February 15. We have a number of comments across the document, but I want to flag several priority issues up front so we can begin substantive discussions promptly. In no particular order:

1\. **Limitation of Liability / Consequential Damages Waiver (Sections 8.1--8.3).** The mutual consequential damages waiver in Section 8.2 is extremely broad and, in our view, inequitable as applied to a licensee that is making a \$14.7 million commitment and transitioning off a legacy platform. We have proposed deletion of the consequential damages waiver in its entirety, or in the alternative, significant carve-outs for data loss, business interruption directly caused by software defects, and costs of re-implementation. Our client\'s exposure in the event of a failed implementation is asymmetrically higher than Meridian\'s, and the risk allocation should reflect that reality.

2\. **Warranty Terms (Section 9.1).** The 90-day limited warranty running from Go-Live is inadequate for an enterprise deployment of this scale. We have proposed a 12-month warranty period with remedies that include, at Licensee\'s election, either (a) repair/replacement, (b) a full refund of all fees paid, or (c) damages for cost of cover. We have also proposed deletion of the warranty disclaimer in Section 9.4 as it pertains to fitness for a particular purpose --- Arcadia is licensing NexusCore specifically for healthcare analytics, and Meridian is fully aware of that particular purpose.

3\. **Acceptance Testing (Section 5.3).** The current draft provides for \"deemed acceptance\" after 15 business days with no written procedures, criteria, or cure period. This is insufficient. We have proposed a detailed acceptance testing framework that includes defined acceptance criteria tied to the Documentation, a structured deficiency notice process, a 30-calendar-day testing window, and a cure period before deemed acceptance can take effect.

4\. **Forum Selection (Section 12.8).** The current draft designates Delaware courts as the exclusive forum. Arcadia is a Texas company, all of its operations are in Texas and Louisiana, the software will be deployed in Texas, and the Meridian team servicing this account is based in Austin. We propose that jurisdiction be vested exclusively in the federal or state courts located in Travis County, Texas.

5\. **Integration Clause (Section 12.1).** We have no objection to a standard integration clause in principle, but want to ensure it is not used as a shield against pre-contractual representations that Meridian\'s sales team has made to our client. We have proposed a carve-out for fraudulent misrepresentations.

We look forward to your response and are available for a call at your convenience to discuss.

Best regards,

Jonathan Breckenridge Partner Wexford Hale LLP 300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500 Austin, TX 78701 (512) 555-8100

*MCS-00004219*

**FROM:** David Arsenault \\\<darsenault@stonebridgecalloway.com\\\> **TO:** Jonathan Breckenridge \\\<jbreckenridge@wexfordhale.com\\\> **CC:** Margaret Calloway \\\<mcalloway@stonebridgecalloway.com\\\>; Patricia M. Espinoza \\\<pespinoza@meridiancloud.com\\\> **DATE:** February 24, 2022, 3:47 PM CST **SUBJECT:** RE: Arcadia Health Systems / Meridian Cloud Solutions --- Draft MSLSA Review **ATTACHMENTS:** Meridian\\*MSLSA\\*Response\\\_v2.docx (152 KB)

Jonathan,

Thank you for the redline and for flagging the priority items. I can confirm that I am handling this matter day-to-day, with Meg Calloway supervising. Patricia Espinoza from Meridian\'s legal department is copied and will be involved as needed.

We have reviewed your redline carefully with our client. Attached is Meridian\'s response markup. Let me address your priority items in turn:

**1. Limitation of Liability / Consequential Damages Waiver (Sections 8.1--8.3).**

This is a non-starter for Meridian, and I want to be transparent about that from the outset so we do not spend weeks negotiating a position that will not change. The mutual consequential damages waiver is a foundational element of Meridian\'s risk allocation framework across all of its enterprise licensing agreements. Meridian licenses NexusCore to clients across healthcare, financial services, and logistics, and the consequential damages waiver ensures predictability and symmetry in risk exposure. We cannot agree to delete it or to carve out categories of consequential damages such as lost profits, business interruption, or re-implementation costs --- each of those is precisely the type of indirect, consequential damage that the waiver is designed to address.

I would note that the waiver is mutual. Meridian also gives up the right to claim consequential damages against Arcadia. The aggregate liability cap in Section 8.1, which limits each party\'s total liability to fees paid or payable during the twelve-month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to liability, provides Arcadia with a meaningful damages recovery path for direct damages in any breach scenario, while ensuring that neither party faces unbounded exposure.

Meridian has not agreed to remove or modify this provision in any of its enterprise license agreements, and it will not do so here. We understand that Arcadia views its risk as asymmetric, but the pricing of the agreement already reflects the risk allocation established by these provisions --- any change to the liability structure would necessarily implicate a repricing of the entire deal.

**2. Warranty Terms (Section 9.1).**

We can discuss the warranty terms, but a 12-month warranty period is not feasible. Meridian\'s standard is 90 days from Go-Live, which is consistent with industry practice for enterprise SaaS deployments. We are willing to discuss the *remedy* structure within that 90-day window --- specifically, we can agree that if Meridian is unable to cure a material nonconformity within 60 days of written notice, Licensee would be entitled to a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees for the unexpired portion of the term. But we cannot extend the warranty period itself, and we cannot agree to a \"cost of cover\" remedy --- that is functionally a consequential damages claim repackaged as a warranty remedy.

Regarding the implied warranty disclaimer in Section 9.4: this stays. The express warranty in Section 9.1 defines the scope of Meridian\'s quality commitment --- the software will perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation. That is a meaningful, testable standard. Meridian is not in a position to warrant fitness for a particular purpose because the ultimate fitness of any software deployment depends on implementation decisions, data quality, system integration, and other factors outside Meridian\'s control. This is especially true here, where Arcadia will be responsible for its own data migration and will be retaining a third-party systems integrator.

**3. Acceptance Testing (Section 5.3).**

This is an area where we can find common ground. We agree that the current 15-business-day deemed acceptance provision could be improved. We are amenable to a 30-calendar-day acceptance testing window following deployment of each module, with acceptance criteria tied to the Documentation. We have proposed language in the attached markup. We do want to preserve the deemed acceptance mechanism --- if Licensee does not deliver written notice of material nonconformity within the 30-day window, acceptance is deemed granted. This is critical for project timeline certainty. We are not willing to add an open-ended cure period that would delay acceptance indefinitely.

**4. Forum Selection (Section 12.8).**

We hear you on forum selection. Meridian\'s preference is Delaware (given its state of incorporation and the Delaware governing law clause), but we understand the practical considerations. We can agree to Austin, Travis County, Texas --- federal or state courts --- as the exclusive forum, provided that the governing law clause (Section 12.7) remains Delaware law. This gives Arcadia the convenience of a Texas forum while preserving the substantive law that Meridian\'s commercial agreements are structured around. Agreed?

**5. Integration Clause (Section 12.1).**

We cannot agree to a fraud carve-out to the integration clause. The integration clause is standard and serves a critical function --- it defines the four corners of the parties\' agreement. Adding a carve-out for \"fraudulent misrepresentations\" would invite collateral disputes over the content and intent of pre-contractual communications and would undermine the certainty that the integration clause is designed to provide. If there are specific representations that Arcadia wants memorialized, the proper mechanism is to include them as express warranties in the agreement itself. We are happy to discuss any additional express warranty language Arcadia would like to propose.

Let me know when you are available for a call. Meg Calloway and Patricia Espinoza can both make themselves available on our end.

Best,

David Arsenault Senior Associate Stonebridge & Calloway LLP 600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 Austin, TX 78701 (512) 555-7200

**FROM:** Jonathan Breckenridge \\\<jbreckenridge@wexfordhale.com\\\> **TO:** David Arsenault \\\<darsenault@stonebridgecalloway.com\\\> **CC:** Margaret Calloway \\\<mcalloway@stonebridgecalloway.com\\\>; Dr. Rachel Okonkwo \\\<rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com\\\>; Martin Schreiber \\\<mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com\\\> **DATE:** February 28, 2022, 10:32 AM CST **SUBJECT:** RE: Arcadia Health Systems / Meridian Cloud Solutions --- Draft MSLSA Review

David,

Thank you for the detailed response. We have discussed internally with our client and I want to share where Arcadia stands.

**1. Consequential Damages Waiver.** We are disappointed but not surprised by your position. Our client understands that this is a deal-level decision. Dr. Okonkwo and Martin Schreiber have reviewed the risk allocation carefully and, after internal deliberation, are prepared to accept the mutual consequential damages waiver **as drafted** --- including the waiver of lost profits, goodwill, data loss, and other intangible losses --- on the condition that the remaining open items are resolved to Arcadia\'s satisfaction. I want to be clear for the record: Arcadia is accepting this risk allocation knowingly and with the benefit of legal counsel, and is relying on the express warranty, acceptance testing, and support provisions to protect its interests. We reserve the right to revisit if the overall package does not come together.

**2. Warranty.** We appreciate the movement on the remedy structure. We can accept a 90-day warranty period with the repair/replace remedy and the 60-day cure period leading to a pro-rata refund as the backstop. However, we want to ensure that \"perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation\" is a meaningful standard. Our redline proposes a definition of \"Material Nonconformity\" as \"any failure of the Software to perform a material function described in the Documentation that materially impairs Licensee\'s use of the Software for its intended purpose.\" Can we agree on that definition?

We will drop the cost-of-cover remedy request and the request to delete the Section 9.4 disclaimer. Martin Schreiber has confirmed that he is comfortable with the implied warranty disclaimer given the express warranty language and the acceptance testing procedures.

**3. Acceptance Testing.** Your proposed 30-calendar-day window with deemed acceptance is acceptable in principle. We have a few refinements:

> \(a\) Acceptance criteria should be defined by reference to specific test scenarios set forth in an exhibit (we will draft Exhibit E with proposed test cases).
>
> \(b\) Written notice of nonconformity must identify the specific Documentation requirement that is not met and include reasonable supporting detail (logs, screenshots, etc.).
>
> \(c\) If Licensee delivers timely notice of a Material Nonconformity, Licensor shall have 15 business days to cure. If uncured, Licensee may reject the module and the parties shall negotiate in good faith regarding remediation.
>
> \(d\) Failure to deliver written notice within the 30-day window constitutes deemed acceptance --- we agree to this mechanism.

We think this framework is fair and gives both sides clarity. It also incentivizes Arcadia to be diligent about testing, which should align with Meridian\'s interest in a smooth Go-Live.

**4. Forum Selection.** Agreed --- Austin, Travis County, Texas (federal or state courts), with Delaware governing law per Section 12.7. We have no objection to this pairing.

**5. Integration Clause.** We hear your position on the fraud carve-out. After discussion with our client, we will withdraw that request. Arcadia accepts the integration clause as drafted. Martin Schreiber has independently verified NexusCore\'s capabilities through reference calls and a proof-of-concept trial, and Arcadia is comfortable proceeding on the basis of the express contractual terms rather than pre-contractual representations.

We are converging. Can we schedule a call for Thursday to finalize the remaining open items? We have mark-ups on several other provisions (data security, indemnification, termination) that I would like to walk through efficiently.

Best,

Jonathan Breckenridge

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

{"file_path": "build_docs.py", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Build the three deliverables for Arcadia v. Meridian:
   motion-to-dismiss.docx, proposed-order.docx, cover-memo.docx
Formatting per Judge Alvarez's Standing Order (12pt TNR, double-spaced, 1in margins,
centered page numbers)."""
import os
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches, RGBColor
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH, WD_LINE_SPACING
from docx.enum.section import WD_SECTION
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

OUT = os.environ.get("OUTPUT_DIR", os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "output"))
os.makedirs(OUT, exist_ok=True)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper functions
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def base_doc():
    doc = Document()
    # Normal style -> Times New Roman 12pt, double spaced
    st = doc.styles["Normal"]
    st.font.name = "Times New Roman"
    st.font.size = Pt(12)
    rpr = st.element.get_or_add_rPr()
    rfonts = rpr.get_or_add_rFonts()
    rfonts.set(qn("w:ascii"), "Times New Roman")
    rfonts.set(qn("w:hAnsi"), "Times New Roman")
    rfonts.set(qn("w:cs"), "Times New Roman")
    pf = st.paragraph_format
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE
    pf.space_after = Pt(0)
    pf.space_before = Pt(0)
    # margins
    for s in doc.sections:
        s.top_margin = Inches(1); s.bottom_margin = Inches(1)
        s.left_margin = Inches(1); s.right_margin = Inches(1)
    return doc

def add_page_numbers(doc):
    """Centered page number at bottom of every page."""
    for s in doc.sections:
        footer = s.footer
        footer.is_linked_to_previous = False
        p = footer.paragraphs[0]
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        run = p.add_run()
        fld1 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld1.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "begin")
        instr = OxmlElement("w:instrText"); instr.set(qn("xml:space"), "preserve"); instr.text = "PAGE"
        fld2 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld2.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "end")
        run._r.append(fld1); run._r.append(instr); run._r.append(fld2)
        run.font.name = "Times New Roman"; run.font.size = Pt(12)

def _set_run(r, bold=False, italic=False, underline=False, size=12, caps=False):
    r.font.name = "Times New Roman"; r.font.size = Pt(size)
    r.bold = bold; r.italic = italic; r.underline = underline
    rpr = r._r.get_or_add_rPr()
    rf = rpr.get_or_add_rFonts()
    rf.set(qn("w:ascii"), "Times New Roman"); rf.set(qn("w:hAnsi"), "Times New Roman")
    rf.set(qn("w:cs"), "Times New Roman")
    if caps:
        c = OxmlElement("w:caps"); c.set(qn("w:val"), "true"); rpr.append(c)

def para(doc, text="", bold=False, italic=False, underline=False, align="left",
         size=12, space_after=0, indent=None, caps=False, line_double=True):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.alignment = {"left":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT,"center":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER,
                   "right":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.RIGHT,"just":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY}[align]
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    pf.space_after = Pt(space_after); pf.space_before = Pt(0)
    if line_double:
        pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE
    else:
        pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    if indent is not None:
        pf.left_indent = Inches(indent)
    if text:
        r = p.add_run(text); _set_run(r, bold, italic, underline, size, caps)
    return p

def runs_para(doc, segments, align="left", space_after=0, indent=None, line_double=True):
    """segments: list of (text, dict-of-flags)"""
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.alignment = {"left":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT,"center":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER,
                   "right":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.RIGHT,"just":WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY}[align]
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    pf.space_after = Pt(space_after); pf.space_before = Pt(0)
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE if line_double else WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    if indent is not None:
        pf.left_indent = Inches(indent)
    for text, flags in segments:
        r = p.add_run(text)
        _set_run(r, flags.get("bold",False), flags.get("italic",False),
                 flags.get("underline",False), flags.get("size",12), flags.get("caps",False))
    return p

def block_quote(doc, text, indent=0.5):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    pf.left_indent = Inches(indent); pf.right_indent = Inches(indent)
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    pf.space_after = Pt(0); pf.space_before = Pt(0)
    r = p.add_run(text); _set_run(r)
    return p

def heading(doc, text, level=1, space_before=6, space_after=2):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    pf.space_before = Pt(space_before); pf.space_after = Pt(space_after)
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE
    pf.keep_with_next = True
    r = p.add_run(text)
    if level == 1:
        _set_run(r, bold=True, underline=True, size=12, caps=True)
    elif level == 2:
        _set_run(r, bold=True, size=12)
    else:
        _set_run(r, bold=True, italic=True, size=12)
    return p

def caption(doc, title, response_note=None):
    """Standard federal court caption block."""
    para(doc, "UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT", bold=True, align="center", caps=True, space_after=0)
    para(doc, "WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS", bold=True, align="center", caps=True, space_after=0)
    para(doc, "AUSTIN DIVISION", bold=True, align="center", caps=True, space_after=6)
    # two-column-ish caption using a table
    t = doc.add_table(rows=4, cols=2)
    t.autofit = True
    cells = [
        ("ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,", "Civil Action No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA"),
        ("Plaintiff,", ""),
        ("v.", ""),
        ("MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,", ""),
    ]
    for i,(l,r) in enumerate(cells):
        lc = t.cell(i,0); rc = t.cell(i,1)
        lp = lc.paragraphs[0]; lp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        lp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        if l:
            rr = lp.add_run(l); _set_run(rr, size=12)
        rp = rc.paragraphs[0]; rp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        rp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        if r:
            rr = rp.add_run(r); _set_run(rr, size=12)
    # merge right column rows for the case number to sit at top
    a = t.cell(0,1); b = t.cell(3,1); a.merge(b)
    para(doc, "Defendant.", space_after=8)
    para(doc, title, bold=True, align="center", caps=True, space_after=4)
    if response_note:
        para(doc, response_note, italic=True, align="center", size=11, space_after=6)
    para(doc, "", space_after=0)
    # horizontal rule
    hr = doc.add_paragraph(); hr.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(2)
    pPr = hr._p.get_or_add_pPr(); pbdr = OxmlElement("w:pBdr")
    bottom = OxmlElement("w:bottom")
    bottom.set(qn("w:val"),"single"); bottom.set(qn("w:sz"),"6")
    bottom.set(qn("w:space"),"1"); bottom.set(qn("w:color"),"auto")
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)

def save(doc, name):
    path = os.path.join(OUT, name)
    doc.save(path)
    print("wrote", path)
    return path

print("helpers loaded")"}

# ===========================================================================
# 1. MOTION TO DISMISS

def build_motion():
